headline: CRUDE OIL DROPS 3% AS GRAINS DRIFT
subheadline: Energy volatility returns while corn holds near $4.44 ahead of planting push.
lead: Crude oil's 3.3% overnight drop to $93.27 signals fresh volatility in input costs just as corn planting season hits full stride. Corn barely budged at $4.44/bu, down just half a cent, while soybeans managed a modest 2.5-cent gain to $11.57/bu. The divergence between energy weakness and grain stability suggests markets are calibrating for different spring narratives.
teaser: Oil's 3% drop creates diesel opportunity as grains hold steady into planting season.

one_number.context: The 3.3% overnight drop breaks crude's recent strength and creates a window for farmers to lock diesel costs before planting demand peaks. With corn holding steady near $4.44, input cost relief could improve planting season margins if you act quickly.

sections.title: Grains & Oilseeds
sections.body: Corn's $4.44/bu holds the line despite minimal pressure, sitting comfortably at 61% of its 52-week range. December corn at $4.71/bu maintains decent carry but nothing exciting. Soybeans showed modest strength with a 2.5-cent bump to $11.57/bu, now at 75% of their 52-week range — suggesting decent upside potential remains. The grain complex is treading water while waiting for weather headlines to drive the next move.
sections.bottom_line: Grains stable but waiting for weather catalyst to break range.
sections.farmer_action: Hold current positions and prep for planting — this stability suggests good underlying support.

sections.title: Livestock & Dairy
sections.body: Yesterday's hog surge momentum faded as lean hogs dropped 0.6% to $107.08, still sitting at 82% of their 52-week range. Live cattle slipped 0.6% to $245.55 but remain near historic highs at 93% of range. Feeders took a harder hit, down 1.1% to $366.43. Class III milk eased 0.2% to $17.37. The livestock complex is consolidating after recent strength, but margins remain historically attractive.
sections.bottom_line: Livestock cooling from highs but margins still historically strong.
sections.farmer_action: Consider locking protection on current positions — volatility suggests more chop ahead.

sections.title: Energy & Inputs
sections.body: WTI crude's 3.3% overnight drop to $93.27 breaks the recent energy rally and creates a diesel pricing opportunity. Natural gas gained 0.3% to $2.75 but remains near historic lows at just 3% of its 52-week range. Soybean meal held steady at $313.90 while soybean oil was flat at $67.26. The energy weakness should translate to lower diesel costs just as planting season intensifies demand.
sections.bottom_line: Oil's drop creates diesel opportunity before spring demand surge.
sections.farmer_action: Lock remaining diesel needs immediately — this dip won't last once planting demand hits.

sections.title: Macro & Trade
sections.body: The dollar index hit an even $100.00 with a slight 0.1% gain, maintaining its recent strength. S&P 500 managed a modest 0.1% gain to $6,616.85, sitting at 81% of its 52-week range. Silver surged 1.3% to $77.44 while gold eased slightly to $4,826. The macro environment remains supportive with managed money (hedge funds) likely reassessing positions after crude's sharp move.
sections.bottom_line: Macro backdrop steady despite energy volatility — dollar strength continues.
sections.farmer_action: Monitor currency moves closely — dollar strength could pressure grain exports.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Energy Moves Matter More During Planting
the_more_you_know.body: A 3% crude oil move might seem disconnected from grain farming, but diesel represents 15-20% of total crop production costs during planting season. Every $10 move in crude translates to roughly 25 cents per gallon at the pump. With 2,000 gallons needed per 1,000 acres, today's oil drop could save $500 per section if you act fast. The window usually closes within days as local demand absorbs the savings.

watch_list.desc: Rain timing for Corn Belt — delays cost 1 bu/acre per day
Crude oil recovery attempt — diesel window may close quickly
Corn sales need to improve with dollar at $100

daily_quote.text: Three sisters — corn, beans, and squash — teach us that the strongest farms grow in community.

source_summary: CBOT/CME futures, EIA energy data, USDA export sales
